text: Robotic Preperitoneal Right Flank Hernia Repair Our patient is a forty eight year old male status post right partial nephrectomy with resection of the right eleventh rib. Past medical history is significant for kidney cancer and hypertension.

The preoperative CT scan shows a large right flank hernia with diastasis of the external oblique muscle and complete disruption of the internal and transversus abdominis muscles, leading to partial herniation of the right lobe of the liver and the right kidney.
These are the preoperative coronal cuts.
The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, preparing for the robotic preperitoneal right flank hernia repair.
The surgeon gains intraperitoneal access by inserting a 12mm trocar through a supraumbilical incision, preparing for the robotic preperitoneal right flank hernia repair.
The laparoscopic evaluation reveals dense adhesions extending from the right rectus muscle to the right flank, visible through the intraperitoneal view.
An eight millimeter subxiphoid trocar is placed, and the robotic surgical system is docked over the patient's right shoulder.
Following adhesiolysis of the anterior abdominal wall, the surgeon places an eight millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant, preparing for further surgical access.
The surgeon places an accessory twelve millimeter trocar in the right mid quadrant, enhancing visualization of the posterior aspect of the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses single arm lysis to clear adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, preparing the area for the insertion of accessory ports to facilitate further lysis of adhesions.
The surgeon reduces the mesentery of the right colon and the kidney from the hernia defect, achieving complete reduction of the hernia contents.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum and creates a preperitoneal flap proximal to the medial aspect of the hernia defect, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, visibly retracting it from the hernia defect.
The posterior aspect of the hernia defect is visible, and the surgeon closes the defect primarily using an OV-lock suture.
The surgeon begins to close the posterior aspect of the hernia defect to offload tension over its widest portion, using sutures to approximate the tissue edges.
The pneumoperitoneum is adjusted to eight millimeters of mercury, reducing intra-abdominal pressure during the hernia repair procedure.
The completed re-approximation of the transversus abdominis and internal oblique muscles is visible, showing the restored alignment of these muscle layers.
A 20x25 cm sheet of physio mesh is placed over the repaired area to reinforce the hernia repair.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the abdominal wall using secure strap tackers, ensuring the mesh is firmly attached to reinforce the hernia repair.
The surgeon re-approximates the peritoneal flap using tackers, securing it in place over the repaired area.
The surgical field shows no visible mesh exposure, indicating successful coverage and placement within the abdominal cavity.

A post-operative CT was performed, secondary to the patient's complaint of a continued persistent bulge. Here we see an anterior seroma behind the external oblique and a pre-peritoneal seroma the musculature remains approximated. Here are coronal cuts.
On the lower left side shows the preoperative CT scan.
